segments.text: Good morning.
Morning, Minister.
We've set aside 15 minutes to
talk about the Agricultural Compounds and Veterinary Medicines
Amendment Bill,
so
hook into it.
Okay. Probably don't need to talk about the problem statement.
I think we all got that from the first reading
and all the evidence you've heard up to now.
In terms of the bill itself, I think what I might just
cover off quickly is
what we're trying to solve is to increase that speed,
make sure these products are getting to market
in a clear
and an improved timeline. We don't want to be spending
years and years. As you heard from some of the submitters on the HASNO,
New Zealand's a very small market in the world.
If it seems like it's too hard a job to do here in New Zealand,
those companies don't come here. Our farmers, growers, don't get
the opportunity to use the latest technology, the newest
drugs, chemicals, whatever. And so it's
important that we're a
market that people want to be involved with, and so
we do need to look at how we can make the process a hell of a lot more
efficient. I think the key thing with the bill is relying on
some of those trusted overseas jurisdictions.
We do have an MOU with Australia, so there's elements
in this bill that's going to make it a lot more
user-friendly in terms of
ensuring that we take advantage
of the work that's already been done in Australia,
to actually get approvals much
faster here in New Zealand.
I think you may have guessed from my question
in the previous thing around the-- and I assume this is going to be a very similar
question that you're going to have placed in front of you in terms
of these timelines. Are they best in primary
legislation? Are they best in regulation?
I've got a feeling that my personal belief and
where we've landed at has been that if we've got them in
regulation, we can target particularly those
new, novel,
ingredients, the stuff that our farmers and
growers need, so we can set precise
timelines for that to be delivered.
At the moment,
we do have it in
primary legislation, and we're not really achieving it anywhere near fast
enough, and it's often we're able to do the simple
stuff really quickly,
but it's that harder stuff that we need to be a bit more focused on.
So my feeling is,
certainly I'd be more
inclined to make sure we stick with where it is
in regulation.
And there's some other matters in there
where the use of
overseas information and stuff, there's-- I
can't remember the wording off by hand, but it's sort of at the
direction of the director general.
And so there's questions there, I think, and the industry's rightly pointing out
that
they are concerned with
that certainty that things will be done.
And so, I would really encourage the committee
to have a good look at
how can we not only speed up this process,
ensure that all the safeguards are in place, but at the same
time, where we position these things, be it primary regulation
or discretion.
There's that trade-off between flexibility and certainty for industry, and
so certainly be interested in any feedback
when you're through this
process, any feedback the select committee has on whether you think
changes could or should be made, to
enable, trying to
find that
ideal balance point between flexibility and
certainty.
Would it be fair to say that we're losing capability
and scientists
offshore because
there's a reticence to invest in R&D
by some of the
companies that once did invest here in New Zealand?
Yeah. You've seen this. And I can't say whether it's
solely attributable to the time
lag that it's taking to get stuff approved by through HASNO and
ACVM,
but it is happening. And so we've got to make sure that
we provide a regulatory environment that encourages
companies to come here to think of- New Zealand
as the innovative agricultural place where they want to be
investing in,
and that they do this research and stuff here in this
country.
We've certainly got the innovative farmers
that'll be taking advantage of it, so I think that's an important thing.
Steve, you had a question. No?
Any other questions? No.
Don't want to know whether Halter's impacted by
Well, go on, tell us.
No.
No. It was funny. That came up-
Sorry. Inside joke. Yeah.
Ah.
Very good. All right. Thanks very much.
No worries.
And-
Like I say, I'm quite interested and, as you go through this process,
any sort of feedback you've got,
I'm more than interested. If you think there's value changes that could be
made to help, not only strengthen the system, speed it up, and give
certainty industry, more than interested in hearing them.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
Thank you. I think the only, the biggest fear of
that is with 10 million dollars.
Yeah. Well, that hopefully the vets will talk about.
Right.
So,
going to move on to the hearing of evidence.
First up is the New Zealand Vegetable Council,
and James and Jay are back.
Yeah. Thank you. Sorry, James had to go back to the farm.
Oh.
So it's just me. But-
Somebody's got to.
There's Jay. Someone's got to work
... So just James.
Yeah.
Yeah. So, I think Jay did a fantastic job this morning to really articulate the
concerns from the vegetable growers' perspectives.
Yeah.
And so this, I guess, he also dovetailed into the ACVM side a bit, so
we'll try and just articulate that position now.
So,
chair, thank you very much for your time today.
My name is James Koufos from the New Zealand Vegetable Council.
We represent New Zealand's vegetable growers, processors, and
exporters across New Zealand, which right now are going through a fairly difficult
time, but we're always encouraged by looking forward.
ACVM, like the HSNO Act, are critically
important for the vegetable sector.
So the key difference, I guess, from the vegetable side of things, compared to
perhaps our larger
colleagues around the primary sector, is just the size of the crops that we're
growing and the label extension. So, we have the
0.1 default MRL in New Zealand, which is superb.
I don't think we could operate without that.
And so we need to make sure we maintain that position around the maximum
residue limits. What we're kind of seeking, I guess, is the ease to be able
to
register products for these crops, for these niche crops.
Because, the default MRL of 0.1 is a great fallback
position, but if the product is actually registered at a different rate, a
different residue limit,
we might be scoring an own goal here in New Zealand where we say, "Actually,
now you've got to have a withholding period of 30 days or 40 days." And so for
some of these crops, like spinach,
that's just not feasible with how quick the crop grows.
No.
So that's where we're getting stuck is saying, "Right, the default is superb,
and we don't want to touch that or look at removing that." But the
headache we've got is getting those label registrations for these
niche crops, and particularly whether, niche like celery or
spinach, et cetera, where there's just no financial incentive for multinationals
to come to New Zealand right now. So, very, very complicated and
tough for our sector, but fundamental as well.
We touched on resistance management earlier on.
So,
without having the ability to rotate these different products
and, yeah, there's a lot of discussion around biopesticides, but also other
novel products coming through that we need to make sure
we have a pathway for future products we don't have a label for yet, or
we don't know about yet. So we're talking mostly about synthetic
chemistry, and we talk about biopesticides, but there are other novel
controls that we need to also keep in mind, keep
open to in the future as well. So just making sure we capture that for
the legislation.
And I guess the other one that we want to highlight is around biosecurity
incursions. So when we do have incursions, yeah, there's rapid
pathways and the likes,
but we think it's more attractive to try and maintain these products for New
Zealand, so we've got them ready to go rather than trying to register them,
and get approval later on. Because that pathway can take
six-plus months and by that stage, whatever we're trying to control is
out of control, out of our hands. So we just need to be mindful
as well about how do we manage
those kind of incursions and make sure we're prepared as best as we can.
Probably the final point
that we want to make is around the legislation is probably a third of
the solution.
The other two thirds are around resourcing and staffing for the
departments, for EPA and MPI, and also making sure that they've
got the right culture, to actually implement the
legislation. We had the HSNO Amendment Act four years ago.
We're going back over through the HSNO Act now, but the culture,
which we're seeing really positive changes right now to actually say we need to be
more permissible, find ways to work through these solutions as well.
We think that's equally as important as the legislation, and we're really
encouraged by both EPA and MPI in
terms of the changes in staff culture to actually say, "We've got to find
solutions here." So, MPI colleagues in particular are looking at the
Codex standards, so falling back to those instead of
having to go to defaults, as well. That's a really sensible approach that
the officials are taking, and so it's something that may not be captured in the
legislation, but we should acknowledge the good work that officials actually are
taking to get there. The resourcing, the backlog
pipeline,
the legislation may help us, but we're still going to have a big backlog to
actually clear. And so we're also supportive of trying to address that
backlog however possible, if it's temporary resourcing,
just so we get rid of that five-year plus backlog and we get back
to where we should be and move forward. Yeah.
So those are the main points that we wish to Just to reiterate, we
think New Zealand's a fantastic place to be growing vegetables.
We want to continue,
and this legislation will help us a lot.
Thank you.
Could you give us an example of something that is covered
by the ACVM Act and not the HSNO Act that you'd
be,
as Vegetable Oil New Zealand-
Yep
... interested in
having a pathway speed up for?
Yeah, sure. So,
it's probably the label extension.
So the one we touched on earlier on, Movento,
in Australia and is registered in New Zealand.
So it can be used in New Zealand already for those crops it's
registered for, and it can be used off-label.
The headache we have is we want to promote the use of some of these products,
but currently we can't do that. So it actually is a really
good IPM product to be able to say it
doesn't harm the beneficial insects in the field and is targeted towards the
thrips.
We can't promote that as industry bodies right now to say, "You should be
using this product because it's softer," because it's not registered on the field
crops that we represent. So that would be an example where we're a little bit
stuck. We kind of-
Mm
... hope that our members come to the appropriate position.
Yeah.
Steve.
Steve, so sup on that. So
for comprehension of the layperson,
you're allowed to use this stuff.
Yep.
It's in New Zealand right now being used. You're not allowed to promote it.
Yeah, correct. So even if it's a softer product-
Yeah
... we can't be promoting something that's not registered for our crops, I guess.
Yeah.
Yeah. And so
why aren't you allowed to promote it?
It's probably one for the officials, but yeah,
we just try to follow the rules, I guess, on it but-
Because there's a regulatory barrier to you promoting.
Yeah.
Yeah.

Right.

Why is that?
Yep. Ugh.

Because you're using it on the foliage of the potato, not on the-

0.1. So yeah, the- ... human

It's systemic, is it?
It's not really, like,
it doesn't

Right

Yep.
Mm.

going straight on the foliage that you eat.
It's not that there's more residue or anything.
It's still just that there's no registration for it, so we default back to that
0.1. Yep. So it's not about the use or crop or, yeah.
Mm.
Okay. Thank you.
Is this what Jay was talking about before with the broccoli and not being able to

Yeah. Yeah. That's right.
Yeah.

Yeah.
Yeah.

Oh, okay.
So his point was, if you're at five parts per million,
for the broccoli,
you can use it till five days in advance.
Whereas if you're off-label and you've got it dropped down to 0.1 now, then

Yeah.
Yep.
But it doesn't actually reflect the safety of the product necessarily.
No, because it's registered already-
Yeah
... in like Australia or something.
Yeah.
So yeah, we just-
Mm

So it's to do with off-label use.
Yeah. So-
For them, yeah.
And on.
And the limits.
Mm.
Yeah, because the default is back to that 0.1.
So if there's a higher threshold for different products, for different crops,
you can go to that threshold.
Would there be-
So it's like a default if it's not registered.
Yeah, I understand.
Yeah.
Would there be-
So it's not actually reflecting the effects.
Yeah. Would there be export
considerations around that-
Um
... do you think?
Yeah. So there are export headaches as well.
So if we export to Europe or UK and to retailers,
they ask us for, "What's your list of registered products in New Zealand?"
And we kind of had to say, "Oh, geez, here's the list.
But we're allowed to use these other ones as well, but you know." And that's a
complicated conversation to have.
Yeah.
I can believe it.
To kind of say, "Oh, these are the ones." And they kind of say, "Oh, you're using
these ones as well." And it's like, "Yeah, like there's a New Zealand pathway
here." So, that's a headache. We would prefer to be
using registered products-
Yeah
... clearly. But,
we also don't want to get rid of that off-label pathway, because that would
really, really damage the efficacy. Yeah.
Steve-
Just for clarity though, the on-label
rules are designed around the-
Principles of what safe ways to use those chemicals, right?
So you're not meant to be using stuff off-label, and once you start using stuff
off-label, you're going outside the parameters that stuff's
been approved as the safe use of that
product.
I've been not really-
No.
Hence using-
No.
It's more like stringent limit that says, "Okay, it's not registered
at five."
Yeah.
You can use the more stringent limit of 0.1 if it's not registered.
Maybe talk about-
Kind of safeguard. Yeah
... is there a reason it's not registered?
It's just, it's still cost to register.
Yeah. So that's the reason.
And so they're cheaper. I think that's what Jay alluded to, too.
Yeah.
That the product, it's not the cost of registration precludes it
being worth it, doesn't it?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay. Any further questions?
No.
Thanks very much, James.
It's okay.
It's complex stuff.
Yeah.
It's complex stuff.
Yeah, it's really detailed, complex, and yeah, we really appreciate the effort to
actually dive into it because it's not easy.
But we've got to find a way through for New Zealand here.
Yeah. What percentage of our fresh vegetables do we export, of the weight, like
what you grow, to your significant-
Prior to two months ago,
would've been more. Now we're probably at 80% domestic for fresh-
I would've even thought that would be higher.
Well, we export onions-
Oh, okay
... about $150 million worth of onions.
We export processed vegetables and frozen fries
to Australia.
Yeah.
Yeah. So-
But fresh is just onions, basically?
Capsicums as well to some markets, and we fly
some stuff into Hong Kong, Singapore, Pacific Islands.
We do a lot of trade because-
Mm
... they can't grow vegetables easily there, so we do a lot of Pacific Island trade
for veggies, yeah. Mm.
Cool. Thank you very much.
Thanks, all.
Thanks, James.
Thank you, mate.
Thank you.
We'll move on to Seed and Grain New Zealand.
Mm.
And we've got Sarah and Cressida back
online.
Hello. Hi.
How are you?
Thanks for having us again. Yes, so I'm Sarah Clark,
the Chief Executive of Seed and Grain New Zealand, and I'm joined
by Cressida Patrick, our Biosecurity and
Technical Manager. So we represent seed
and grain companies and also ag chem companies who are
supplying chemicals to the seed and grain industry.
Cressida and I are going to do a tag-team submission again.
I'm conscious that you've heard our context earlier this
morning-
Mm-hmm
... when we addressed the HASNO bill.
But for the record on ACVM submission, I am going to
repeat much of our context to set the scene.
A key point is that our industry relies on
chemicals to produce seeds and grains important to New Zealand's
economy. Crop failures are our future if we
can't access appropriate chemicals.
So our members are producing grains, including wheat, barley, and
oats, and these are used for human consumption and also
our animal feed. And our members are producing the seeds for
sowing for the domestic,
excuse me,
and the international markets.
It includes grass seed, clover seed, forage,
oil seeds, and vegetable seeds. Our industry does have
a reputation in the global seed trade for
providing very high-quality seeds,
and we want to keep it that way, because we're
producing 40 to 50% of the world's carrot seed and
60% of the world's radish and clover seed.
Why are chemicals important to our industry?
Well, the seeds for sowing are treated with a light coating
of agrichemical before they're sown, and it really does
make a big difference to the health
of the crop. It gives the crop a really healthy start because it's
protecting the seed and the young shoots and roots in the
soil from pests and diseases. And then, of course, we're using
chemicals during the growth of the crop,
for controlling weeds and pests and diseases, all the way through to
seed set.
So yeah, without access to the right chemicals at the right
time, farmers and seed companies can't produce that
high-quality, high-purity seed and grain that is
demanded. There's a world global demand for our seed because
of our reputation for doing such a good job.
And hence, because chemicals are so important, the proposed changes
to the ACVM Act are something that we want to comment
on. There are some aspects that we think can be further
improved.
I spoke earlier
in the HASNO bill hearing about the impact of
the regulatory settings on our industry,
and those comments are equally relatable to the
ACVM Bill, so I will emphasize them again.
The regulations have a direct impact on the sustainability and
productiveness of arable farms and seed companies.
And
a key reason why that matters is the codependency between the arable
sector and the other primary sectors.
So any positive or negative impact on arable
farms does flow through all the way to our
dairy farms and sheep farms, whose export
products are underpinning wealth generation in New Zealand.
And secondly, the regulations will
impact our ability to supply seeds back to the
international market.
And the key issue there is that the EU in particular is
prohibiting agrochemicals faster than we can approve
chemicals that they do allow. So,
we will be unable to use new or novel chemistries unless our
approval process is quick pace. I am going to
hand across to Chris. She's got three points, technical
points that she would like to address. Thank you.
Sarah. So as with the Hasno Amendment Bill, we're supporting
modernizing the ACVM framework. We believe
this will provide a more proportionate risk-based regulatory system,
and we support increasing alignment with the trusted international regulators.
These are reforms that our sector's been advocating for for many years.
The submission has three key points.
The first one is that we support the proposal to allow the Director-General to
exempt lower-risk agricultural compounds from registration
requirements.
We consider exemptions are an important part of modern risk-based
regulatory frameworks and lower-risk products, such as
biostimulants, which are often cited as
a crop,
a probiotic rather than a chemistry,
is considered a lower-risk compound and shouldn't face the
same regulatory burden as a higher-risk compound.
We think moving these exemptions from Cabinet to the Director-General will make the
process more efficient and responsive, and
we consider this as a very positive move.
However, we would like to urge that transparent criteria is
used, and that clear evidence requirements and clear
explanations when an application is declined,
is provided by the Director-General to the
appropriate people. We believe this will help ensure the
consistency, fairness, transparency, and
confidence in the decision-making process that
you will have heard a lot of the applicants are reliant on.
Secondly, we believe the bill should go further in recognizing assessments
undertaken by the trusted overseas regulators.
We would like to acknowledge the work that the New Zealand Food Safety has done in
the Trans-Tasman pilot for manufacturers and agrochemicals
to support registration in both countries, and we would support more
initiatives like this.
Where a product has already undergone a rigorous scientific assessment by a
recognized regulator such as Australia, Canada, EU,
US,
these assessments should form the primary basis for the decision-making in
New Zealand, unless there's a clearly identified New Zealand-specific
consideration.
As currently drafted, we consider that the bill gives significant
discretion to the Director-General, but doesn't necessarily provide that
feedback loop to the applicant
that provides the certainty that they need.
The overseas assessments may be considered, but there's no
clear expectation that they will be relied
upon. We recommend stronger requirements for transparency,
including clear criteria for recognition
decision-making, and again, we'd like to emphasize that
explanation should be provided back to the applicant where an overseas
assessment is not relied upon.
Thirdly,
we consider that there is the missed opportunity to
focus on that, establishing the dedicated pathway for
biopesticides and as James alluded to, as well
as other newer novel chemistry,
newer techniques that we may not necessarily be aware of.
The New Zealand framework treats many biopesticides in a
similar manner to conventional synthetic pesticides, despite
their generally lower risk profiles.
We believe that this creates higher compliance cost, regulatory
uncertainty, and obviously the lengthy approval time frames.
As alluded to in our earlier presentation, the biopesticides and
other newer techniques are becoming increasingly important
offshore as the older synthetic chemistries are
being phased out. We believe that ensuring a
framework that supports these newer technologies
can help manage resistance, reduce reliance on the older
chemistry, and support access to the domestic markets that we need to
continue accessing.
I'll just give two quick examples.
So domestically, we've had the loss of chlorpyrifos, and we've got the
upcoming loss of diazinon, two of the
main controls for grass grub, a hugely important New Zealand
endemic pest species that's not present anywhere else,
that has a huge impact on the arable sector.
We are going to become increasingly reliant on these
biopesticides such as BioShield and Verterblast to help
control New Zealand grass grubs.
Unfortunately, these biopesticides take a number of years to
actually establish
and grow
in the actual fields themselves and also limit the
synthetic chemistries that can be used when they have been applied.
So there's some considerations that our farmers will need to
actually consider
in an upcoming on-the-ground example that's going to
happen.
Globally, the EU again is one of our main markets, and as
they phase out the synthetic chemistry, we will not
be able to supply treated seed that has the older chemistries that
are available to us.
Other jurisdictions such as Australia, Canada, US, and EU have
developed more tailored and proportionate pathways for
biopesticides and some of those other lower-risk compounds,
and we recommend the bill considers including an enabling provision that
allows these dedicated pathways in New Zealand.
In closing, Seed and Grain support the overall direction of the
ACVM Amendment Bill, and we welcome your commitment to working on
the framework.
We consider this as a long overdue opportunity, and
we are excited to see genuine reform,
but we do consider further work is needed.
We encourage the committee to strengthen the bill by providing greater
certainty to the applicants around the
recognition of trusted international assessment, and also we
really encourage that transparency
for applicants to ensure those business cases remain
justified and eligible within New Zealand.
New Zealand growers and seed companies need a regulatory system that
can keep pace with innovation without jeopardising New
Zealand's human health or the environment.
By getting these settings right, we believe we will support a more productive,
sustainable, and internationally competitive future for our sector
and for the wider agricultural economy.
Thanks again for your opportunity to
speak to our submission, and we'd welcome any questions.
Thank you. We've got Steve.
